content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Why industry voices are pointing to a new agent category — and what makes one actually work.
In 2026, a new agent category is showing up in industry projections.
It has a name: Code Clean-up Agents. It sits between AI code generation and shipping. It does the work that nobody wants to do by hand, and that nobody is going to skip past, because the alternative is shipping AI-generated code that nobody fully trusts.
This post is a map of the category: what it does, why it exists now, where it sits in the SDLC, and what makes one actually work instead of being another AI-coding-agent with a marketing label.
What the category actually does
A Code Clean-up Agent handles three jobs that AI generation leaves behind.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Refactor AI-generated code into something maintainable. The first PR out of an agent looks fine. The second PR, against the first, looks weird. The fifth PR, against all of them, looks like it was written by three different developers on three different days. A Clean-up agent reads the accumulated diff, finds the patterns that don’t agree with each other, and rewrites them so a human can read them later.
Hunt vulnerabilities in code that was assembled faster than it was reviewed. SQL injection, path traversal, leaked secrets, insecure deserialization, BOLA on a new endpoint. These didn’t disappear when AI coding arrived. They got faster, because the agent copies patterns from the open web without checking if the patterns were safe to begin with. A Clean-up agent runs deterministic security passes on every file it touches and surfaces findings before the diff is approved.
Recent work is starting to put numbers on this: a 2026 study of vibe-coded applications found a measurable security debt against the apps developers actually ship (arXiv:2606.23130), and a parallel study on LLM-assisted PQC development reported secure coding drift over the lifetime of the codebase (arXiv:2606.19474). The drift is structural, not a one-off bug.
Standardize conventions, naming, and patterns across AI-written files. A senior reviewer enforces the codebase’s house style by feel. An agent has to enforce it by rule, and only if the rule is grounded in what the IDE already knows about the project. A Clean-up agent should be able to read the existing code style and apply the same conventions to AI-written additions, so the codebase still reads like one codebase.
Three jobs, one category. None of them are part of “AI code generation” as it’s usually sold. All of them are part of what makes the AI-modified SDLC actually work.
Why this category exists now
AI generation moved a stage of the SDLC from days to seconds. It did not move the next stage, and the next stage is where the cost now sits.
A feature that used to take three days to write now takes thirty minutes with an agent. The PR goes up. Review starts. The reviewer spends three hours reading, re-running, hunting vulnerabilities, and re-deriving things the IDE already knew. Net: a few hours saved on generation, a few hours lost on review, a vulnerability sometimes slipped through.
Industry voices are pointing at the gap and calling it a category. Code Clean-up Agents are the agents built to fill the gap.
The logic is simple. If code is cheap to generate, the bottleneck moves from generation to everything that has to happen after generation. Whoever owns that bottleneck owns the 2026 niche.
Cost moves with that bottleneck too. In Tanmay Bansal’s account of Microsoft and Uber’s AI-agent spending, Microsoft reportedly began steering internal engineering teams from Claude Code toward cheaper tools after an initial rollout. Uber’s example is sharper: Bansal writes that 84% of its engineers had adopted AI coding tools, yet the company exhausted its entire 2026 budget for them in four months. One engineering leader reportedly spent $1,200 in tokens during a two-hour demo.
Those examples do not show that coding agents stopped being useful. They show why usage is the wrong success metric on its own. An agent can be widely adopted, produce working code, and still fail the spreadsheet if every difficult task expands into another round of reading, rewriting, testing, and retrying. Bansal estimates agent workflows can consume 5 to 30 times more tokens than a chat exchange, with runaway debugging loops climbing far higher. The relevant question is no longer just did the agent finish? It is what did a verified result cost, and where should the loop have stopped?
The research community is converging on the same gap from a different angle. LLM-agent benchmarks for architectural code smell repair (arXiv:2605.07001) and LLM refactoring studies (arXiv:2606.21171, arXiv:2606.07341) all describe the same shape: AI generates, then humans struggle to clean up after it. Code Clean-up Agents are what fills that shape.
The SDLC is shifting
The old cycle was plan → code → test → deploy.
The AI-modified cycle added a generation stage: plan → AI-generate → code → test → deploy. The 2026 cycle adds a clean-up stage in the middle:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

plan → AI-generate → Code Clean-up → test → deploy
That middle stage is what was missing. AI generation made code cheap. Code Clean-up is what makes it shippable.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most “AI coding agents” today skip this stage and ship whatever the generation produced. The market is starting to notice. Code Clean-up is the stage where the agent has the IDE’s facts and can read the diff, refactor against the codebase, run deterministic checks, and surface everything to the developer before the diff is approved.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What “Code Clean-up” actually delivers
Cleanup agent delivery 25 75 5x and 3x speedup itemized receipt
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Three measurable outcomes, not vibes.
Fewer rounds between PR and merge. A Clean-up agent catches the issues a senior reviewer would have flagged: naming, null-checks, missing tests, conventions that don’t match the codebase. The PR lands with fewer comments, fewer back-and-forth commits, and fewer review meetings.
Fewer bugs reaching production. Security passes, inspections, and runtime instrumentation aren’t optional on a Clean-up agent. They run on every change, the same way they run on every save in the IDE, so the diff is reviewed for vulnerabilities before the developer is asked to review it.
One codebase, not five. AI-written additions match the conventions of the file they were added to. Imports are ordered the way the project orders them. Names follow the project’s naming rules. Methods follow the project’s test patterns. A reviewer can’t tell from reading the diff which lines were AI-written and which weren’t, and that’s the point.
The numbers from one engineering team put it like this: about 25% of a developer’s day is spent writing code; the other 75% is the work around it. A Code Clean-up Agent targets that 75%. The reported speedup is roughly 5× on the work around the code, and about 3× on overall output, while the developer keeps control of the code and their grip on the repo. (source)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A Code Clean-up Agent that ships autonomously isn’t a Code Clean-up Agent. It’s an AI coding agent with a different label.
The category’s hard part is control. The agent proposes changes. The developer accepts them. The diff is itemized so a reviewer can read what was changed, why it was changed, what it cost, and which tests ran on it.
The itemized receipt is the design center of the category. Every change is logged with the model used, the time spent, the tokens burned, the cost in minutes, the files touched, and the tests run. Accepted by you, auditable by everyone. (source)
This is where Bansal’s idea of “Containment Engineering” becomes useful for code clean-up. His prescription is concrete: route simple tasks to cheaper models, cap retry loops, stop a session when it crosses a token budget, prune failed context before another attempt, and send the task to a human when the limit is reached. Applied to a Clean-up Agent, those controls answer two different risks at once. IDE inspections and test runs constrain what the agent may claim about the code. Loop caps, model routing, and live cost accounting constrain what it may spend while reaching that claim.
The human review is not a temporary compromise in that design. It is the final verification step and the financial kill switch. When the next retry costs more but adds no new evidence, the agent should stop and show its work: the attempted changes, the failed tests, the remaining inspection findings, the tokens used, and the files touched. That gives the developer enough information to continue, redirect, or reject the run without paying for an open-ended loop.
A Code Clean-up Agent without that receipt is just a faster way to ship code nobody fully understands. With the receipt, it’s the missing stage of the 2026 SDLC.
This is also the framing the agent-research community is converging on: knowledge-based pull requests as a trusted workflow for agent-mediated code collaboration (arXiv:2606.26721), where the agent’s diff is structured for human review, not optimized for autonomous merging. The itemized receipt isn’t a UX feature; it’s the trust boundary between an autonomous coder and a Code Clean-up Agent.
Explyt is an IDE-native Code Clean-up Agent
Explyt lives inside the JetBrains IDE, where it can read the symbol index, run the inspections, decompile dependencies, hit the debugger, and instrument runtime calls. It proposes changes, itemized with the receipts above. You accept. A reviewer audits.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The category is new. The agent category’s first benchmarks will be set by whoever ships the first production-grade implementation of it. The IDE is the natural place to land it, because the IDE is the only tool that has the facts to do the work honestly.
Try Explyt 5.15: https://explyt.ai
FAQ
What’s a Code Clean-up Agent? An agent category introduced in 2026 industry projections that handles refactoring, vulnerability hunting, and standardization of AI-generated code between AI generation and shipping.
Where does it sit in the SDLC? Between AI-generate and test, as a new middle stage of the development cycle.
What makes one actually work? A review-shaped loop over the diff: AI code review, IDE inspections, SAST security passes, project-grounded style enforcement, verification test runs, and an edit-scoped, itemized diff the developer approves. Without those, it’s just an AI coding agent with a new name.
Is Explyt a Code Clean-up Agent? Yes. Explyt is built as an IDE-native Code Clean-up Agent that proposes, doesn’t decide, and ships with itemized receipts.
How much does it cost? Community is free with your own API key. Personal and Pro are paid tiers by monthly minutes. Enterprise is custom by contract. First 30 days are free on paid tiers.
